So there's two concepts of intrinsic evaluation and extrinsic evaluation.
So intrinsic evaluation.
I'm evaluates the quality of the output itself. And so that would be like asking human directly about how good is this output.
It's intrinsic evaluation is evaluating output quality by its utility.
And so just to give one example, if you can evaluate large language model summaries through question answering accuracy.

And so you can take the output of an LLM and feed it through a question answering model and see whether you're able to answer questions based on this and that kind of gives you a better idea of whether the summary required incorporates requisite information.
But if you think about anything in LLM can be used for usually it's part of a bigger system. So you've been evaluated as part of that bigger system.
The problem with this is it's a very indirect way of assessing things. So like let's say your QA model is just bad.
How can you disentangle the effect of the LLM summary versus the QA model that's not trivial things to do. So ideally like a combination of these two is probably the best way to go.

Okay so yeah moving on to the learning part so now I'd like to talk about learning and the first thing I'll cover is error risk and so basically the way we calculate error is we generate an output and we calculate its badness.
And so generating the output could be our next it could be sampling it could be anything else like that.

And we calculate its badness which is one minus in about which could be like how bad is the output if you're you have a badness measure or it could be one minus the evaluation square to calculate its badness.

And this is divided into the error and generally what you want to do is you want to minimize error because in the end you're going to be deploying a system that outputs you know one thing and you're going to want that to be as good a thing as possible.

But the problem with this is there's no easy way to actually optimize this value in especially in a text generation style setting but even in the classification setting we can't easily maximize error because if you look at the.

If you look at the surface of the error at some point you're going to have a non-differentiable part when you take the dark max and or when you do sampling or anything like that so you're not going to be able to do gradient based optimization.

So what we do normally is we instead calculate something called risk and what risk looks like is we talked a little bit about minimum base risk for decoding but this is for a training time and what it looks like is it's essentially the expected error of the output and the expected error of the output includes a probability and the objective function here.
